title: Iran-backed militias strike Saudi Aramco facility in Yanbu, Brent jumps 6% as Strait of Hormuz fears intensify
topicContext: EUR/USD is the most-traded currency pair in the world. It is sensitive to relative monetary policy between the European Central Bank and the Federal Reserve, eurozone vs U.S. growth differentials, risk-on/risk-off flows, and geopolitical events that affect the dollar's safe-haven status. The pair typically falls (USD strengthens) during global risk-off episodes. Major support levels in the current range: 1.0820 (recently broken), 1.0780, 1.0720. Resistance: 1.0920, 1.0980. The pair has been in a sustained downtrend since the U.S. strikes on IRGC positions in eastern Syria on April 7.
body:
Iran-backed Houthi fighters and Iraqi militia groups launched a coordinated drone-and-missile strike against the Saudi Aramco oil processing facility in Yanbu on the Red Sea coast in the early hours of Wednesday, Saudi state media confirmed. The strike caused a major fire at one of the facility's stabilization columns and forced an emergency shutdown of approximately 1.4 million barrels per day of crude processing capacity, according to Saudi oil ministry officials.

The attack is the most significant escalation in the regional crisis that began on April 7 with U.S. airstrikes against Iranian Revolutionary Guard Corps positions in eastern Syria — strikes themselves launched in retaliation for a drone attack on a U.S. base in northeast Syria that killed three American service members. Iran's foreign minister had pledged at the time that Iran would 'respond at a time and place of our choosing,' and Wednesday's strike on Yanbu appears to be that response, although Iran has not formally claimed responsibility.

Markets reacted sharply to the news. Brent crude jumped more than 6% in early Asian trading to above $98 per barrel, the highest level since November 2022 and an extension of the move that began Monday. WTI traded above $94. The U.S. dollar index gained another 0.8%, taking its week-to-date move above 2.5%, while EUR/USD broke decisively below the 1.0820 support level that had held since February, trading at 1.0775 in Asian hours.

Gold rose to a fresh record above $2,410 per ounce. The Japanese yen and Swiss franc both gained against the dollar even as the dollar strengthened against most other currencies — a pattern consistent with classic safe-haven flight rather than a pure dollar story. Volatility indices spiked: the VIX traded above 22 in extended hours, and the MOVE index of bond volatility hit its highest level since the regional banking stress of 2023.

'This is the kind of compounding shock that markets have been pricing as a tail risk for two days,' said one London-based macro strategist. 'The question now is no longer whether Iran will retaliate but how broadly and how often. The Strait of Hormuz risk is no longer hypothetical. Insurance rates on Gulf shipping doubled overnight.'

U.S. defense officials told Reuters that the administration is 'considering all options' and that additional military assets are being moved into the region. The White House has not ruled out further direct strikes against Iranian targets in Iraq or Syria. Saudi officials confirmed they are 'in close consultation' with the U.S., UAE, and Bahrain about the regional security situation.

The European Central Bank declined to comment on market conditions but is widely expected to acknowledge the inflation risk from sustained higher oil prices at its meeting next week. Several Federal Reserve officials, speaking on background, expressed concern that the oil price spike could complicate the path to rate cuts the central bank has been signaling for later this year. Money markets pulled forward bets on a Fed cut to 'at most one' in 2026, down from 'one to two' before this week.

Market analysts now point to a clear bearish setup for EUR/USD over the next several sessions. With 1.0820 support broken and the eurozone disproportionately exposed to higher oil prices, the pair has limited fundamental reasons to bounce. Targets being mentioned by traders include 1.0720 (the next major support) and, in a more aggressive scenario, parity (1.0000) within several weeks if the regional crisis broadens further. The base case among most strategists, however, is for the pair to find support around 1.0720 and consolidate, awaiting clarity on whether U.S.-Iran tensions stabilize or escalate further.
